title: Need for Cognition Example Dataset
text: following vignette showcases use elfs() applied data Need Cognition scale (Cacioppo & Petty, 1982), using data collected originally analyzed Hussey Hughes (2020). First, dataset loaded.

title: Need for Cognition Example Dataset
text: first model, conduct simplistic model scale (18 items load onto factor). resulting elfs() output contains information fitted model. initiating specific calls, can output information regarding: latent factor scores ultimately decided , can easily extracted incorporated dataset (, original dataframe):

title: Need for Cognition Example Dataset
text: simple model can expanded, firstly testing measurement invariance. Within nfc_data, “sex” binary variable denoting sex participant. Separate models group can run using following: resulting information regarding measurement invariance can viewed: desired, individual latent factor scores can extracted group.
code: nfc_invar <- elfs(data = nfc_data,                   f1_cols = starts_with("nfc"), f1_name = "NeedforCognition",                   meas_invar = "sex",                   chrome_bypass = T) #Raw model nfc_invar$group_models #> [[1]] #> lavaan 0.7-2 ended normally after 32 iterations #>  #>   Estimator                                         ML #>   Optimization method of freedom                               304 #>   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.000 #>   Test statistic for each group: #>     m                                         1942.722 #> title: Need for Cognition Example Dataset
text: Finally, dynamic fit indices can also requested: Resultant information updated model cutoffs can displayed:
code: nfc_dynamic <- elfs(data = nfc_data,                     f1_cols = starts_with("nfc"), f1_name = "NeedforCognition",                     dynamic = T,                     chrome_bypass = T) nfc_dynamic$dynamic #> Your DFI cutoffs:  #>             MAD   Sim. MAD CFI   RMSEA 90% CI #> Consistent  0.00  0.00     0.999 0.006 0.009  #> Specificity                95%   95%          #>                                               #> Close       0.038 0.036    0.933 0.052 0.054  #> Sensitivity                95%   95%          #>                                               #> Fair        0.05  0.047    0.892 0.067 0.069  #> Sensitivity                95%   95%          #>                                               #> Mediocre    0.06  0.057    0.848 0.082 0.084  #> Sensitivity                95%   95%          #>  #> Estimator: ML  #> Sample Size: 6649  #>  #> Empirical fit indices:  #>  Chi-Square  df p-value   SRMR    CFI   RMSEA RMSEA 90% CI #>     4110.06 135       0  0.047  0.894   0.067        0.068 #>  #>  Notes: #>   -'Sensitivity' is % of incorrect models identified by cutoff while rejecting <5% of correct models #>   - If sensitivity is <50%, cutoffs will be supressed #>  #>   -'90% CI' column is RMSEA cutoff, including sampling variability #>   - Only compare upper limit of 90% RMSEA confidence interval to '90% CI' column #>   - Do not compare RMSEA point estimate to '90% CI' column #>  #>   -'MAD' is the desired mean absolute discrepancy #>   -'Sim. MAD' is the MAD that was achieved in the simulations #>   - Values may diff when avoiding non-positive definite matrices #>

title: Short Dark Triad Example Dataset
text: following vignette showcases use elfs() applied data Short Dark Triad (SD3; Paulhus & Jones, 2001), collected Open-Source Psychometrics Project. First, dataset loaded; responses filtered retain complete data, converting responses 0 NA.

title: Short Dark Triad Example Dataset
text: first model, can conduct simplistic model SD3. Treating factors Dark Triad (Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy) separate factors measured 9 items . following model, latent factors allowed covary (.e., oblique rotation). resulting elfs() output contains information fitted model. initiating specific calls, can output information regarding: latent factor scores ultimately decided , can easily extracted incorporated dataset (, original dataframe):

title: Short Dark Triad Example Dataset
text: additional specification measured variables treated ordinal variables (.e., using DWLS estimator, instead ML) can specified using typical ordered = T syntax lavaan. Note ordered = T, nonconvergence tau-equivalent model much likely, restricting potential comparisons. resulting tables displaying model fit modified accordingly:
code: #column selection can take in a traditional character vector, or use "starts_with()" or "ends_with()" SD3_elf_ordered <- elfs(data = SD3_data,                         f1_cols = starts_with("M"), f1_name = "Machiavellianism",                         f2_cols = starts_with("N"), f2_name = "Narcissism",                         f3_cols = starts_with("P"), f3_name = "Psychopathy",                          ordered = T,                          chrome_bypass = T) #> Warning in ifelse(as.numeric(cfa_matrix[1, 3]) < 0.001, "< .001", paste0("= ", #> : NAs introduced by coercion #> Warning in what: elfs: Evaluating and Estimating Latent Factor Scores — elfs-package
title: elfs: Evaluating and Estimating Latent Factor Scores — elfs-package
text: Automated evaluation/estimation latent factor scores, building 'lavaan'. Provide data wide format, column names factor, receive comprehensive information regarding model fit, reliability, factor correlations, measurement invariance, dynamic fit indices.

title: elfs: Evaluating and Estimating Latent Factor Scores — elfs-package
text: Maintainer: Neil Hester neil.hester@uwaterloo.ca (ORCID) Authors: Neil Hester neil.hester@uwaterloo.ca (ORCID) Shawn Yee s6yee@uwaterloo.ca (ORCID)

what: Estimates and evaluates latent factor scores for scales — elfs
title: Estimates and evaluates latent factor scores for scales — elfs
text: Estimates evaluates latent factor scores scales

title: Estimates and evaluates latent factor scores for scales — elfs
code: elfs(   data,   f1_cols,   f2_cols = NULL,   f3_cols = NULL,   f4_cols = NULL,   f5_cols = NULL,   f6_cols = NULL,   fg_cols = NULL,   f1_name = NULL,   f2_name = NULL,   f3_name = NULL,   f4_name = NULL,   f5_name = NULL,   f6_name = NULL,   fg_name = NULL,   ordered = FALSE,   missing = "listwise",   dynamic = FALSE,   meas_invar = NULL,   modify = NULL,   lfs_method = NULL,   lfs_transform = TRUE,   chrome_bypass = FALSE )

title: Estimates and evaluates latent factor scores for scales — elfs
text: data data.frame object f1_cols, f2_cols, f3_cols, f4_cols, f5_cols, f6_cols Character vector(s) listing column names included () latent factor. Compatible dplyr::starts_with() code. f1_cols required. fg_cols Character vector listing column names included additionally-specified "general factor" (.e., bifactor models). f1_name, f2_name, f3_name, f4_name, f5_name, f6_name Optional names (character) specified latent factor. fg_name Optional name (character) "general factor" (see argument fg_cols, defaults "FactorG"). ordered Whether treat measured variables ordinal (vs. continuous; see lavaan::sem()). FALSE default. missing treat missing data (listwise deletion default, see lavaan::lavOptions() alternatives) dynamic Whether estimate dynamic fit indices (McNeish & Wolf, 2023). use, download latest development version Github using pak::pkg_install("dynamic") devtools::install_github("melissagwolf/dynamic"). FALSE default. meas_invar Character indicating column name split-conducting measurement invariance across categorical variable. NULL default, assuming measurement invariance analysis. modify Additional character character vector attached SEM specification prior model fitting (e.g., specifying correlated residual variances) lfs_method Character string indicating method estimating latent factor scores (details default information, see lavaan::lavPredict()) lfs_transform Whether transform extracted factor scores match model-implied mean variance-covariance (details, see lavaan::lavPredict()). TRUE default, following best practice latent factor scores used subsequent regression analysis. chrome_bypass Whether bypass chrome-screenshot method displaying results running elfs(). FALSE default.
